summary: Otis Whitfield was named after a Whitfield from South Carolina history. I recall that he fought a duel with one of the Tillmans, specifically Pitchfork Ben Tillman. The name Whitfield carries a legacy that ties back to the region's past, and I always found it interesting how family histories connect to larger historical narratives.

My husband's family lived in Summerville, South Carolina, and when they sold their property, we brought wisteria from there to remind us of home. South Carolina's history is rich, and it even connects to my family's past through the Whitfield name. I also remember Sherman burning Columbia in February 1865, which was a significant event in the state's history.

Pitchfork Ben Tillman was quite a notorious figure, and I remember hearing that Otis Whitfield fought a duel with him. He was considered a bad member, and his reputation preceded him. His name often came up when discussing South Carolina's turbulent political past.

Sherman's March to the Sea impacted my family's property directly. Sherman burnt the courthouse in Columbia on February 18, 1865, and his troops passed right by where I was raised. This event marked a significant moment in our local history and shaped the landscape and memories of the area.

I grew up about five miles out on Highway 21, which is the old road to Charlotte, North Carolina. Most of my family lived there except for my sister, who moved farther away. Charlotte was always a point of reference for where we lived and traveled.

My sister lived in Birmingham, Alabama, for a time, and I remember the civil rights struggles there, especially with the police chief Bull Connor. Birmingham was a place of significant tension and activism during those years, and it was connected to people I knew through the Southern Negro Youth Congress.

My sister moved to Ann Arbor, Michigan, where she and her husband have lived for many years. He is now retired, and they remain there. It was quite a distance from where I grew up, but it shows how our family spread out over time.

I attended the Southern Race Relations Conference held in Durham in October 1942. The meeting was significant for discussing issues like voting rights and segregation. It was held at North Carolina Central College, and many notable figures like Gordon Hancock and P.B. Young were involved. I am one of the few remaining attendees from that conference.

Durham was the site of the Southern Race Relations Conference in 1942, which I attended. It was also home to North Carolina Central College and the North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, both important institutions in the community. I have connections there and know people like Clemmons who worked with the insurance company.

summary: Jordan Hancock was one of the people listed as attending the Southern Race Relations Conference, but I know he has passed away. I knew most of the attendees from that time, and Jordan was among them. His contributions were part of the collective effort for civil rights during that era.

Charles Johnson was a great man who attended the Southern Race Relations Conference. He wrote the carefully worded statement opposing segregation but suggesting a strategic approach to it. I knew him well, and his work was significant in shaping the group's stance during the 1940s.

Luther Foster was another attendee of the Southern Race Relations Conference, but he has since passed away. I remember him as part of the group working toward racial justice during that period. His efforts contributed to the broader movement in the South.

H. B. Butler was a sweet man who attended the Southern Race Relations Conference, but he is no longer living. I recall him fondly as part of the group striving for progress during the 1940s. His presence was important in those meetings.

J. A. Bacote was listed among the attendees of the Southern Race Relations Conference, but I believe he has passed away. I wasn't entirely sure at first, but checking with Benedict College confirmed it. He was part of that important circle of civil rights activists.

Virginia is where James Jackson and his wife Esther live now. They were active civil rights workers and involved with the Southern Negro Youth Congress. I remember them as fine young people, and their work was important in the movement.

James Jackson was head of the Southern Negro Youth Congress in Birmingham and a dedicated civil rights worker along with his wife Esther. They are still living, likely in Virginia, and I remember them as outstanding activists. Their work was crucial during the struggles we faced in the South.

The Southern Negro Youth Congress was an organization deeply involved in civil rights work, especially in places like Birmingham. James Jackson was head of the youth congress there, and many others I knew were connected to it. It was a key group in the fight against segregation and injustice.

Bull Connor was the police chief in Birmingham and a notorious figure during the civil rights struggles. He was a 'bull' in the worst sense, opposing the movement and enforcing segregation harshly. We had a lot of trouble with him and his police force during those times.

Rufus Clement was someone I knew but he has passed away. He was part of the circle of people involved in civil rights and education during my time. His contributions are remembered even though he is no longer with us.

North Carolina Central University, then known as North Carolina Central College, was the site of the Southern Race Relations Conference in 1942. James Sheppard was president of the college at the time. The university was a hub for important discussions on race relations and civil rights.

James Sheppard was president of North Carolina Central College during the 1942 Southern Race Relations Conference. He has since passed away, but I remember him well from that period. He was a key figure in education and the civil rights movement in Durham.

summary: The North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company was an important institution in Durham connected to the African American community. Clemmons, who was originally from Charleston and worked there, is now retired but still lives in Durham. The company was a center for many community activities and meetings.

Clemmons is a retired man still living in Durham who was connected to the North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company. I spoke with him recently and he looked well. He attended meetings related to race relations and was part of the community's efforts toward progress.

summary: Gordon Hancock was the initiator and one of the leaders of the Southern Race Relations Conference. He played a major role in organizing and guiding the discussions. I knew him well and respected his leadership in the movement during the 1930s and 40s.

P. B. Young was another leader alongside Gordon Hancock at the Southern Race Relations Conference. Together they helped steer the organization and its goals. I knew both men and their efforts were central to the civil rights work of that time.

I was given the name Helena Modjeska after a famous Polish actress from that time. My mother's baby sister thought it was a beautiful name, so I carried it with me all these years. Helena Modjeska was well known then, and her name has a special place in my family history.

During the 1942 Southern Race Relations Conference, there was a heated debate about whether to come out fully against the Jim Crow laws. Some argued that opposing segregation outright would lose white support, which was necessary for progress. I would have been on the side demanding a firm stance against segregation, but the group decided to oppose it in principle while living within its boundaries for the time being.
